qa_pairs.question: Why is it important to understand the various filesystems on SciNet?
qa_pairs.answer: Understanding the various filesystems, and how to use them properly, is critical to optimizing your workflow and being a good SciNet citizen.

qa_pairs.question: What type of high-performance filesystem is predominantly used on SciNet?
qa_pairs.answer: The filesystems on SciNet, with the exception of /archive, are GPFS (IBM General Parallel File System).

qa_pairs.question: What are the performance characteristics of GPFS?
qa_pairs.answer: GPFS is a high-performance filesystem that provides rapid reads and writes to large datasets in parallel from many nodes.

qa_pairs.question: How does GPFS perform when accessing datasets with many small files?
qa_pairs.answer: The GPFS filesystem performs quite poorly at accessing datasets which consist of many, small files.

qa_pairs.question: Can you provide an example of GPFS performance difference between large and small files?
qa_pairs.answer: Reading data from one 16MB file is enormously faster than from 400 40KB files on GPFS.

qa_pairs.question: What is the blocksize for the scratch and project filesystems?
qa_pairs.answer: The blocksize for the scratch and project filesystems is 16MB.

qa_pairs.question: Why are small files wasteful of space on SciNet's GPFS filesystems?
qa_pairs.answer: Small files are quite wasteful of space because the blocksize for the scratch and project filesystems is 16MB.

qa_pairs.question: What is an inefficient I/O strategy for multi-process jobs on SciNet?
qa_pairs.answer: Having each process write to a file of its own is not a scalable I/O solution for multi-process jobs, as a directory gets locked by the first process, causing others to wait or the filesystem to time out.

qa_pairs.question: What are the recommended solutions for I/O in multi-process jobs to avoid performance issues and crashes?
qa_pairs.answer: Consider using MPI-IO, which allows files to be opened simultaneously by different processes, or using a dedicated process for I/O to which all other processes send their data, and which subsequently writes this data to a single file.

qa_pairs.question: What is the primary purpose of the /home ($HOME) filesystem?
qa_pairs.answer: /home is intended primarily for individual user files, common software or small datasets used by others in the same group, provided it does not exceed individual quotas.

qa_pairs.question: Is the /home filesystem accessible on compute nodes?
qa_pairs.answer: /home is read-only on the compute nodes.

qa_pairs.question: What is the /scratch ($SCRATCH) filesystem primarily used for?
qa_pairs.answer: /scratch is to be used primarily for temporary or transient files, for all the results of computations and simulations, or any material that can be easily recreated or reacquired, as well as intermediate steps in a workflow.

qa_pairs.question: What should users do with their final, long-term results from /scratch?
qa_pairs.answer: Once users have their final results that they want to keep for the long term, they should migrate them to /project or /archive.

qa_pairs.question: Is /scratch regularly purged or backed up?
qa_pairs.answer: /scratch is purged on a regular basis and has no backups.

qa_pairs.question: When should users consider using the burst buffer (/bb) instead of /scratch for intermediate workflow steps?
qa_pairs.answer: Users should consider the burst buffer (/bb) for intermediate steps if their workflow induces too much I/O or too many small files on the disk-based /scratch storage pool.

qa_pairs.question: What is the intended use for the /project ($PROJECT) filesystem?
qa_pairs.answer: /project is intended for common group software, large static datasets, or any material very costly to be reacquired or re-generated by the group.

qa_pairs.question: What is the expected immutability of material stored on /project?
qa_pairs.answer: Material on /project is expected to remain relatively immutable over time.

qa_pairs.question: Why should temporary or transient files not be kept on /project?
qa_pairs.answer: Temporary or transient files should not be kept on /project because high data turnover induces stress and unnecessary tape consumption on the TSM backup system, due to backup retention policies and extra versions kept.

qa_pairs.question: What happens if a user renames top directories on /project?
qa_pairs.answer: Renaming top directories is enough to trick the system into assuming a completely new directory tree has been created and the old one deleted, impacting backup systems.

qa_pairs.question: What is the consequence for users who abuse the /project filesystem by using it as /scratch?
qa_pairs.answer: Users abusing the /project filesystem and using it as scratch will be flagged and contacted.

qa_pairs.question: Is the /project filesystem available to all users on Trillium?
qa_pairs.answer: No, on Trillium /project is only available to groups with RAC (Resource Allocation Competition) allocation.

qa_pairs.question: What is /bb ($BBUFFER)?
qa_pairs.answer: /bb, the burst buffer, is a very fast, very high-performance alternative to /scratch, made of solid-state drives (SSD).

qa_pairs.question: When should a user request the burst buffer (/bb)?
qa_pairs.answer: Users may request the burst buffer if they anticipate a lot of IOPs (Input/Output Operations) or when they notice their job is not performing well running on scratch or project because of I/O (Input/Output) bottlenecks.

qa_pairs.question: What is /archive ($ARCHIVE) used for?
qa_pairs.answer: /archive is a 'nearline' storage pool used to temporarily offload semi-active material from other filesystems, as part of a regular workflow, or when hitting quotas on scratch or project.

qa_pairs.question: How long can material remain on HPSS (the technology behind /archive)?
qa_pairs.answer: That material can remain on HPSS for a few months to a few years.

qa_pairs.question: Is the /archive filesystem available to all users on Trillium?
qa_pairs.answer: No, on Trillium /archive is only available to groups with RAC (Resource Allocation Competition) allocation.
